channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: How to bolster the arsenal of democracy
item.description: America’s new defence-tech industry should be a model for Europe

item.title: How Iran and the United States Are Planning Their Next Moves
item.description: Karim Sadjadpour on the extended cease-fire and continued blockade of the Strait of Hormuz.

item.title: What Happens if the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire Collapses?
item.description: The truce is hanging by a thread despite Trump’s extension.

item.title: Hungary Drops Veto on Massive EU Loan to Ukraine
item.description: Reopening the Druzhba pipeline was the final hurdle stopping Budapest from ending its opposition.

item.title: Is a Private-Credit Crisis Imminent?
item.description: The rapid expansion of opaque and minimally regulated lending outside the traditional banking system over the past 15 years largely flew—as intended—under regulators’ radar. Now, however, signs of trouble in shadow banking are multiplying, with rising redemptions and notable fund failures stoking fears of a 2008-style crisis. Though we might not be headed toward a meltdown just yet, regulators, especially in the United States, are turning a blind eye to the growing risks.

item.title: The Big Picture
Pakistan Gambled on Mediation
item.description: As U.S.-Iran talks falter, the political and economic risks are rising for Islamabad.

item.title: America’s descent into state capitalism is exaggerated
item.description: Particularly by the state

item.title: How Europe regulated itself into American vassalage
item.description: The road to economic serfdom

item.title: Can the Bundeswehr fight?
item.description: In an interview with The Economist, Germany’s top general promises his forces will be “war-ready”

item.title: Can the Germans fight?
item.description: In an interview with The Economist, Germany’s top general promises the Bundeswehr will be “war-ready”

item.title: Crypto-miners are quietly colonising computers
item.description: Hijacking processing power cuts costs

item.title: How to stop colour-blind grouse flying into ski lifts
item.description: Use signage they can see

item.title: Is a Perfect Financial Storm Gathering?
item.description: Between the explosive growth of shadow banking, the relentless rise in public debt across major economies, and stalled implementation of the regulatory reforms enacted after the 2008 financial crisis, those charged with maintaining financial stability have much reason to worry. The window to do something about it is closing fast.

item.title: Donald Trump’s deportation machine is innovating
item.description: What lies behind the administration’s deals with African countries

item.title: Morocco’s New Model Economy
item.description: Africa’s future will not be written by countries waiting for perfect conditions. It will be written by countries like Morocco, which made the most of its undesirable post-colonial inheritance to become not only an economic success story but a potential model for others to emulate.

item.title: A US Strategy For Defending Taiwan – Before a War
item.description: Insights from Eyck Freymann.

item.title: An extended ceasefire over Iran, but for how long?
item.description: The answer may depend on how much economic pain each side can endure

item.title: Does Private-Credit Smoke Mean Financial Fire?
item.description: Does the rapid growth in non-bank lending pose a threat to the stability of the financial system? While the current chair of the US Securities and Exchange Commission insists that it does not, there are growing signs that something is rotten in the state of private credit.

item.title: The Race for the Next U.N. Chief Kicks Off
item.description: The selection process for António Guterres’s successor begins with a grilling for four contenders.

item.title: Where Is the China-Honduras Relationship Headed?
item.description: Despite the campaign rhetoric, so far, there are no concrete signs of a rapprochement with Taiwan by Honduras’ new government.

item.title: Will AI Solve Rich Countries’ Debt Woes?
item.description: Although many commentators believe that AI has the potential to supercharge economic growth and solve our most vexing problems, such optimism underplays the risks. If anything, the technology is likely to cause profound—and costly—problems on the way to the halcyon future.

item.title: Why Trump Should Make China-US Relations Great Again
item.description: A country insulates itself from China at its own peril, at least economically. The road to making America great again must go through Beijing.

item.title: A Good Ban, Done Wrong: How to Accelerate Lasting and Just Solutions Amid Bali’s Waste Crisis
item.description: Closing Suwung landfill is the right decision. But the government has yet to provide sustainable alternatives for Bali's waste.

item.title: Russia to Halt Shipments of Kazakh Oil to Germany on May 1
item.description: “There have been no official statements from the Russian side yet, but from unofficial sources, we know it's true,” Kazakh Energy Minister Yerlan Akkenzhenov said of the impending stoppage.

item.title: What if China Succeeds?
item.description: Why Beijing’s success spells doom for everyone else.

item.title: The curious rise of Chinese whisky
item.description: Why one tipple is thriving in an otherwise dispirited market

item.title: The Intersection of Organ Trafficking and Bonded Labor in Pakistan
item.description: Pakistan’s brick kiln industry in particular is known for the peshgi system, which leaves workers forever trapped in debt – and vulnerable to coerced organ donations.

item.title: Escaping the IMF Austerity Trap
item.description: There is ample research showing that growth-oriented strategies accompanied by redistribution policies are the most promising path to recovery across the Global South. Rather than imposing cookie-cutter austerity programs, the International Monetary Fund should be following the evidence.

item.title: How a Sudanese militia built a military and economic empire
item.description: Three years into Sudan’s civil war, the Rapid Support Forces has grown into a regional power

item.title: The Andaman Sea Tragedy: Why Rohingya Youth Continue to Risk Death to Leave the Camps
item.description: Despite repeated loss of life, Rohingya migration by sea continues.

item.title: Decoupling and South Korea’s New Capitalism
item.description: The South Korean stock market’s remarkable recent performance reflects not only successful government initiatives but also the rapid rise of individual investors. Now, instead of always reinvesting profits, Korean firms are delivering more short-term value to shareholders—a change that could help keep capital in the country.

item.title: American Jews’ Netanyahu Problem
item.description: Political polarization, the collapse of institutional gatekeepers, and social media’s amplification of fringe voices were always going to put pressure on American Jews. But Benjamin Netanyahu's alliances and policies have compounded these forces, putting US Jews in the crossfire of partisan politics and Middle East adventurism.

item.title: Why the Junta Released Myanmar’s President
item.description: Aung San Suu Kyi’s fate remains uncertain.

item.title: America Should Be Israel’s Partner, Not Its Patron
item.description: The pro-Israel case for ending U.S. aid

item.title: US Strategic Incoherence and the Iran War
item.description: Views of the Iran crisis vary from the perspectives of U.S. allies in Asia, Central Asia’s oil exporters, and South Asia’s perpetual foes, India and Pakistan.

item.title: The Ugly Side of Eswatini-Taiwan Relations
item.description: Even before President Lai’s Eswatini visit was canceled, labor rights lawsuits, abuse allegations, and free speech concerns were bubbling under the surface.
